repo_notes.content: Zwei CLI is a fork of opencode with a PhD / Supervisor dual-agent split. PhD writes code, Supervisor verifies it. The two run in physically isolated sessions with independent skill sets, one-way (asymmetric) memory, and write/test separation (PhD has no bash and no read access to tests). The orchestrator escalates to the Supervisor only when needed via three modes: single (PhD only), dual (PhD + Supervisor every round), auto (PhD first, Supervisor on demand â€” skipped when test gate passes). Package on npm: @zweicli/cli, command: `zwei`. Written in TypeScript on Bun, built as a single AOT-compiled binary via `bun build --compile`. Internal identifiers still say Student/Supervisor; README and user-facing UI say PhD/Supervisor.

pages.title: Zwei Overview
pages.purpose: What Zwei is, why it exists, and how it differs from single-agent CLIs. Explains the asymmetric writer/verifier split, the three modes (single / auto / dual), and the context-pollution and Goodhart problems the split is designed to avoid.

pages.title: Getting Started
pages.purpose: Install via `npm install -g @zweicli/cli`, run `zwei`, walk through the first prompt. Covers provider setup, model selection, and what the default landing state looks like (dual route, `auto fast`).

pages.title: Core Concept: Single / Auto / Dual
pages.purpose: The three execution modes selected via `/agents`. single = PhD only (no verification). dual = PhD + Supervisor every round. auto = PhD first, Supervisor invoked on demand (the auto-test-gate skips Supervisor when tests pass, cutting wall time ~60% on easy rounds). Trade-offs between cost, latency, and verification strength.

pages.title: Execution Flow
pages.purpose: How a task flows through a dual round: user prompt â†’ PhD session (write-only, no bash/tests) â†’ optional test gate â†’ Supervisor session (reads PhD's code + test output, emits structured JSON verdict) â†’ orchestrator decides: pass â†’ done; fail â†’ feed `repair_hint` back to PhD; loop bounded by maxRounds. Covers queueing follow-up prompts (pending queue consumed on run termination).

pages.title: PhD & Supervisor Roles
pages.purpose: Responsibilities and constraints of each role. PhD: editor/write tools only, no bash, no test-file reads â€” cannot self-verify. Supervisor: reviews only, sees PhD's code and test runner output, returns structured JSON (`pass` / `fail` + `main_issue` + `evidence` + `repair_hint`). Explains session-level permission isolation and why this isn't prompt-only.

pages.title: Asymmetric Memory
pages.purpose: Memory flows one way, by design. PhD never sees Supervisor's reasoning â€” only the structured verdict crosses the boundary. Supervisor sees PhD's code and test output. Prevents PhD from Goodharting the verifier by chasing its phrasing. Describes how this is enforced at the session boundary.

pages.title: Write / Test Separation
pages.purpose: Why PhD has no bash and no read access to test files. Self-verification is physically impossible, and the writer's context stays clean â€” no tool traces, no test output, no file dumps polluting it. Extends to how this interacts with compaction: stale tool outputs from earlier rounds get evicted between rounds.

pages.title: Compaction & Context Control
pages.purpose: The fork's `SessionCompaction.prune` extensions (`protectTokens` / `minTokens` / `protectTurns`), why upstream's unconditional 40 K protect window was the wrong default for mid-size sessions, and how the dual loop calls compaction between rounds with a 2 K protect window to evict stale tool-call outputs.

pages.title: Independent Skills
pages.purpose: Each role loads its own skill set â€” nothing shared by default. PhD isn't distracted by review tooling, Supervisor isn't tempted to reach in and edit. Covers how skills are declared and how they're filtered per role.

pages.title: Slash Commands
pages.purpose: TUI slash commands that matter for the dual workflow: `/agents` (pick mode Ă— role), `/model` (both), `/model1` (PhD only), `/model2` (Supervisor only), `/clear`, `/clear1` (PhD), `/clear2` (Supervisor). Includes the `fast` vs `plan` variant distinction.

pages.title: Plan Mode
pages.purpose: The `plan` agent variant: read-only planning phase before switching to `fast` (execution). Combined with `dual` / `auto` / `single`, gives six total options (e.g. `dual plan`, `auto fast`). Covers the plan â†’ execute handoff.

pages.title: Queue & Follow-up Prompts
pages.purpose: How submitting a follow-up prompt while a dual run is live works: the text is stamped to the parent session (visible in the transcript, like opencode's QUEUED badge) and pushed into a local queue. When the current run transitions from live (starting/running) into a terminal status (pass/fail/error/aborted/budget_exceeded), the next pending prompt fires as a fresh round on the same child sessions.

pages.title: Configuration
pages.purpose: Config options: model per role, default mode, compaction thresholds, maxRounds. Covers auto-upgrade settings (disabled/notify/on, targets `@zweicli/cli` via npm/bun/pnpm).

pages.title: Architecture
pages.purpose: Internal structure: SessionDualAgent orchestrator, the three sessions per task (parent + student + supervisor), structured JSON contracts at the round boundary, telemetry events on the bus, auto-test-gate, interaction with opencode's Session service. Notes where the fork diverges from upstream opencode.
